segments.s100-g9: بگویی is a literary/poetic hortative/optative construction (ب + گویی) used to introduce reported speech or a hypothetical remark — here best read as “one might say” or “you might say.” که introduces the content of that reported clause. لشکر is the grammatical subject “the host(s).” ترا is the clitic object (تو reduced to -ت) plus the object marker را (here written separately) marking “you” as definite object. خواستند is the past tense, 3rd-person plural of خواستن, “they wanted/sought,” agreeing with لشکر.
segments.s100-g10: همی is an archaic/poetic adverb that adds immediacy or emphasis (often rendered “at once,” “straightaway,” or “indeed”); in epic it commonly precedes material it scopes over. تخت شاهی is a compound: تخت “throne” modified by شاهی (here adjectival/possessive sense, “royal” / “of kings”). بیاراستند is the literary past, 3rd-person plural of (ب)آراستن, “they adorned/arrayed,” the verb agreeing with the previously mentioned لشکر.
segments.s100-g11: که again introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause. در + خورد forms a prepositional description: در خورد تاج کیان = “in the inner/smaller part (the inner circlet) of the crown of the Kians”; خورد here is an archaic adjectival use meaning ‘inner/smaller/low(er).’ جز...کس is the construction meaning “no one except… / none but …”: جز تو کس literally “except you, any person.” Word order is compact and typical of epic diction.
segments.s100-g12: نبینیم is the negative present/jussive (ن + بینیم), 1st-person-plural form used narratively as “we see not / there is none whom we see”; this is an archaic/poetic choice instead of modern نمی‌بینیم. شاها is the vocative “O king!”; تو is the emphatic pronoun “you,” and فریادرس is a predicate noun meaning “rescuer/deliverer.” The clause is effectively verbless for the predicate: “we see no one but you — O King, you [are] the rescuer.”
segments.s100-g13: تهمتن is an epic epithet/name (Tahmatan / “the mighty one”) functioning as the subject. زمین را is the definite object with the object marker را. به + مژگان is an instrumental phrase “with (his) eyelashes” (به here marking instrument/means). برفت is a literary past (prefixed form of رفت) — a 3rd-person singular past used in epic diction to indicate the action he took upon the earth; the exact verbal shade is archaic/poetic (see senses mapping).
segments.s100-g14: کمر برمیان بست is a common idiomic sequence: کمر “waist/girdle,” برمیان (adverbial) “around / about the middle,” and بست (past 3sg of بستن) “he tied/girded” — together: “he girded his waist.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” چون باد is a simile “like the wind.” تفت here is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning roughly “he rushed/sped” (its ordinary modern senses don't match; treat as poetic verbal usage), completing the simile “and like the wind he sped.”
segments.s100-g1: "به رستم چنین گفت فرخنده زال" is a slightly inverted, poetic Persian word order: the prepositional object 'به رستم' (to Rostam) comes first, 'چنین گفت' (said thus) is the reporting phrase, and the speaker 'فرخنده زال' (the epithet + name) follows the verb. 'چنین گفت' is a fixed formula meaning "thus he said."
segments.s100-g2: The particle 'که' introduces reported speech (here a command). 'برگیر' and 'بفراز' are archaic/poetic imperative forms built on stems (with the prefix ب- that appears in older Persian imperatives); they are coordinated by 'و'. The direct object 'کوپال' is a named weapon and 'یال' (mane/crest) is the thing to be raised.
segments.s100-g3: 'برو' is an imperative 'go'. The following noun 'تازیان' is the intended addressee/target (the Arabs) without an overt object marker — normal in epic/poetic style. 'تا ...' here expresses extent 'as far as / up to', so 'تا به البرز کوه' means 'as far as Mount Alborz' (note classical order 'البرز کوه' = 'Mount Alborz').
segments.s100-g4: 'گزین کن' is a two-part imperative: the verb 'گزین' (choose/select) followed by the common auxiliary/imperative 'کن'. 'یکی' here means 'one' / 'a single', modifying 'لشکر' (army). 'همگروه' follows the noun as an attributive (poetic order) meaning 'of the same group' / 'allied'.
segments.s100-g5: In 'ابر کیقباد آفرین کن یکی' 'ابر' is a poetic epithet used before the king's name (literally 'cloud' but here functioning as an honorific/metaphor). 'آفرین کن' is an imperative phrase 'offer praise/acclamation' (آفرین = acclamation; کن = do), and 'یکی' qualifies it as 'one' / 'once' / 'a single (acclamation)'.
segments.s100-g6: 'مکن' is a negative imperative 'do not'. 'پیش او' is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in his presence' or 'before him'. The sequence 'بر درنگ اندکی' uses 'بر' + the verbal noun 'درنگ' (delay, tarrying) plus the adverb 'اندکی' (a little) to mean 'do not tarry even a little' — literally 'do not (on) delay a little'.
segments.s100-g7: 'به دو هفته باید که' is a temporal frame plus necessity: 'به دو هفته' = 'within/for two weeks' and 'باید که' introduces a clause meaning 'it is necessary/it must be that'. 'ایدر' is an adverb 'here'. The word 'بوی' as printed in the lexicon list would normally mean 'smell', but in this line it functions as an older/poetic form related to existence/presence (not the literal 'smell'); none of the provided noun senses fits the usage exactly.
segments.s100-g8: 'گه و بیگه' is a fixed poetic pair meaning 'now and then / at times'. 'از' is the preposition 'from'. 'تاختن' is the verbal noun 'raiding/charging'. 'نغنوی' is an archaic verbal form based on غنودن ('to slumber/cease'); here it carries a negative/cessation sense (i.e., 'do not cease/rest') so 'از تاختن نغنوی' reads roughly 'do not stop from raiding' — in other words, keep raiding intermittently.

sentences.s100-s5: One might say that the hosts sought you and at once arrayed the royal throne.
sentences.s100-s6: For within the inner circlet of the Kayan crown we see no one but you; O King, you are the rescuer.
sentences.s100-s7: Tahmatan touched the earth with his eyelashes, girded his waist, and like the wind sped away.
sentences.s100-s1: The auspicious Zal said to Rostam thus: "Take up the Kupāl and raise the crest (mane)."
sentences.s100-s2: Go (to) the Arabs up to Mount Alborz; choose one allied army.
sentences.s100-s3: Praise (or acclaim) Kay Qobad once; do not linger even a little in his presence.
sentences.s100-s4: It is required that for two weeks you remain here, now and then — do not cease from raiding.
